rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is a benign, painless, stable pigmentary macule with no infective, necrotic or systemic features. It does not require emergency or same-day care. The correct disposition is a routine (1–2 week) in-person GP or dermatology review to confirm the diagnosis, exclude a fungal/chemical mimic that cannot be judged on video, and discuss elective treatment. Pure self-care with no review (T0) would under-manage the residual diagnostic uncertainty; anything urgent (T3+) would be over-triage driven by the patient's panic — the very error the in-person clinician in the source note explicitly prevented.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The white patch breaks open into a bleeding ulcer or open sore
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Attend an emergency department promptly if this occurs
escalation_edges.rationale: Ulceration or breakdown of the lesion would signal a process other than benign vitiligo and needs urgent assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the white patch ever breaks open into a bleeding sore or ulcer, go to an emergency department to have it looked at.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The area becomes hot and very painful, or a severe rash spreads rapidly with a high fever
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek emergency care the same day if these develop
escalation_edges.rationale: Heat, severe pain, rapid spread and fever suggest an aggressive soft-tissue infection requiring urgent treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the area becomes hot and painful, or you get a spreading rash with a high fever, that's different — go to an emergency department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The patch develops a thick, crusty scale, or the pale patches begin to spread rapidly over a few days
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: See a GP within 24–48 hours if this develops
escalation_edges.rationale: New scale or rapid spread would raise a treatable fungal infection and warrants prompt (not emergency) GP reassessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the patch starts to flake with a thick scale, or the pale areas spread quickly over a few days, book a GP appointment within a day or two.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Overwhelming, unmanageable distress or any thoughts of self-harm
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day help (GP, crisis line, or ED) if distress becomes unmanageable
escalation_edges.rationale: The acute component is disproportionate anxiety; if distress becomes unmanageable or there are thoughts of self-harm, that itself needs urgent support.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel you can't cope with how distressed you are, or you have any thoughts of harming yourself, please reach out today — call your GP, Lifeline on 13 11 14, or go to an emergency department.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Acute catastrophic health anxiety with an active urge to self-present to the emergency department tonight
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Calm, explicit reassurance and a clear routine follow-up plan are therapeutic here and directly prevent an inappropriate emergency presentation; the safety-net thresholds must be stated plainly so the patient knows exactly what WOULD warrant urgent care.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a harmless loss of skin pigment, not an infection or a burn. Stop scrubbing the area, apply a plain moisturiser, and try not to keep checking it tonight. Book a routine GP or dermatology appointment in the next 1–2 weeks to have it confirmed and to talk about treatment or camouflage. You do not need an emergency department tonight.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP or dermatology appointment within 1–2 weeks
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This can safely wait for normal clinic hours; there is no need to seek after-hours or emergency care for the patch itself unless one of the warning signs below develops.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the periorificial and fingertip depigmentation, the painless non-scaly character, the reactive scrubbing erythema, the negative chemical exposure, and the significant health anxiety — for GP confirmation, mimic exclusion, thyroid screening and treatment discussion.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the lesion as definitely benign with pure self-care and no in-person review, ignoring that a fungal or chemical mimic cannot be excluded on video.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a benign, painless, stable pigmentary macule to urgent care, an emergency department or an ambulance under the patient's panic — the primary error this case tests.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Fine scale, texture and induration cannot be assessed on video, so a superficial fungal (pityriasis versicolor) or chemical mimic cannot be fully excluded remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Give reassurance about the clearly benign, non-emergency features while being honest about the limits of a remote assessment, and arrange a routine in-person examination to confirm.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Resolving the uncertainty by either extreme — declaring it definitely benign and arranging no review, or escalating to emergency care to 'be safe'.
